examples.title: Algorithm: Interrupt-Driven I/O Mechanism
examples.description: Demonstrates how the CPU handles multiple devices using interrupts, allowing it to continue processing while waiting for I/O
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates the interrupt-driven I/O mechanism. When a device needs attention, it raises an interrupt signal. The CPU saves its current state (registers, program counter), identifies the interrupt source via an interrupt vector, disables further interrupts to prevent nesting, executes the appropriate Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) to handle the device, restores the previous state, and resumes the main program. This allows the CPU to efficiently handle multiple devices without busy-waiting.

examples.title: Python: DMA Controller Simulation
examples.description: Simulates Direct Memory Access (DMA) controller handling block transfers between device and memory without CPU intervention
examples.code:
import time
import threading
from enum import Enum

class DMAState(Enum):
 IDLE = 0
 INITIALIZED = 1
 TRANSFERRING = 2
 COMPLETE = 3
 ERROR = 4

class DMAController:
 """Simulates a DMA Controller for efficient block transfers"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 self.state = DMAState.IDLE
 self.source_address = 0
 self.destination_address = 0
 self.transfer_count = 0
 self.memory = bytearray(65536) # Simulated memory
 self.cpu_working = True
 
 def initialize_transfer(self, source, dest, count):
 """CPU initializes DMA transfer with parameters"""
 self.source_address = source
 self.destination_address = dest
 self.transfer_count = count
 self.state = DMAState.INITIALIZED
 print(f"[DMA] Initialized: {count} bytes from {source} to {dest}")
 print("[CPU] Continuing execution while DMA handles transfer...")
 
 def perform_transfer(self):
 """DMA controller performs the actual transfer"""
 self.state = DMAState.TRANSFERRING
 print(f"[DMA] Starting transfer of {self.transfer_count} bytes...")
 
 # Simulate bus mastering and data transfer
 for i in range(self.transfer_count):
 # DMA reads from source and writes to destination
 data = self.memory[self.source_address + i]
 self.memory[self.destination_address + i] = data
 time.sleep(0.001) # Simulate transfer time
 
 if i % 100 == 0:
 print(f"[DMA] Transferred {i}/{self.transfer_count} bytes")
 
 self.state = DMAState.COMPLETE
 print(f"[DMA] Transfer complete!")
 return True
 
 def cpu_work_simulation(self):
 """CPU performs other work while DMA transfers"""
 work_items = ["Calculate spreadsheet", "Render graphics", "Parse network packets"]
 for task in work_items:
 print(f"[CPU] Processing: {task}")
 time.sleep(0.5)
 print("[CPU] All background tasks complete")

# Demonstrate DMA operation
def demonstrate_dma():
 print("=" * 60)
 print("DMA Controller Demonstration")
 print("=" * 60)
 
 dma = DMAController()
 
 # Initialize some data in memory (simulating device buffer)
 for i in range(500):
 dma.memory[1000 + i] = ord('A') + (i % 26)
 
 # CPU initializes DMA transfer
 dma.initialize_transfer(source=1000, dest=50000, count=500)
 
 # Start DMA transfer in separate thread (simulating hardware)
 dma_thread = threading.Thread(target=dma.perform_transfer)
 dma_thread.start()
 
 # CPU does useful work concurrently
 dma.cpu_work_simulation()
 
 # Wait for DMA to complete
 dma_thread.join()
 
 # CPU is interrupted only at the end
 print(f"[CPU] Interrupt received: DMA transfer finished!")
 print(f"[CPU] Verifying {dma.memory[50000:50010]}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 demonstrate_dma()
examples.explanation: This Python simulation demonstrates how DMA allows efficient block transfers. The CPU initializes the DMA controller with source, destination, and transfer count, then continues executing other tasks. The DMA controller performs the actual data transfer between the device and memory, reading each byte and writing it to the destination. The CPU is only interrupted twice: once when the transfer completes (or errors). This simulates real-world DMA operation where the CPU is freed for computation while bulk data transfers occur autonomously.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for DMA transfer, O(1) for CPU overhead per transfer
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for memory buffer, O(1) for DMA controller state

examples.title: C: Interrupt Priority and Daisy Chain Implementation
examples.description: Implements interrupt priority handling using daisy-chain configuration to service higher priority devices first
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates interrupt priority handling using a daisy-chain configuration. Devices are arranged in priority order (power failure highest, disk lowest). When multiple interrupts occur, the system scans through the priority chain and services the highest-priority device first. Once a higher-priority interrupt is being serviced, lower-priority interrupts are blocked until the ISR completes. This mimics hardware daisy-chaining where the interrupt acknowledge signal propagates through devices until one claims it.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of devices in daisy chain
